[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

<references />

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11590920]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:28, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

* [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese Mythology]]

[[:en:Shinto|Shinto]]'''Shinto Five Texts''' (shinto gobusho) are the fundamental scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (Watarai Shinto), comprising the following five texts:
* ''{{ill|Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja|天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshoza Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Shidai Ki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Account of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja|伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nisho Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Denki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("Records of the Enshrinement of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja|豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Kotai Jingu Go Chinza Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Enshrinement of Toyouke Grand Shrine")
* ''{{ill|Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja|造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zo Ise Nisho Daijingu Hoki Honki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ("The Fundamental Record of the Treasures of the Two Ise Grand Shrines")
* ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'' ("The Chronicle of Yamatohime no Mikoto")

Although these texts claim to have been compiled before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually written during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by {{ill|Watarai Yukitada|ja|度会行忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai Yukitada|1=Watarai Yukitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17217919}} and other priests of the {{ill|Outer Shrine|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]].

== Overview ==
Research from the fields of {{ill|History|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]] suggests that the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the Outer Shrine, compiled these texts to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to be equal or even superior to the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}—the deity enshrined at the Outer Shrine—with [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi-no-Kami]], arguing that Toyouke possessed a higher, more universal divine status than Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史 |date=2020th year 3月25日 |publisher=（株）山川出版社 |pages=82-84 |author=原田実}}</ref>

Ise Shinto itself was formulated as a critique of the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Suijaku]] theory through its doctrine of {{ill|Shinpon Butsuseki|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsuseki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}, which asserted the primacy of Shinto over Buddhism. However, it has been suggested that the logic underlying these foundational texts of Ise Shinto was influenced by Buddhist thought, particularly the interpretations found in works such as ''{{ill|Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|1=Yamato Katsuragi Hozan Ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}'', a Shinto theory developed from the perspective of [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]].

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Tōshōgū Shrine]] in [[:en:Owari Province|Owari Province]], vehemently criticized the Shinto Five Texts as {{ill|forgeries|ja|偽書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/forgeries|1=forged document|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42757690}}.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}

'''The Five Texts of Shinto''' (神道五部書, *Shintō Gobusho*)

== Overview ==
The *Five Texts of Shinto* are the foundational scriptures of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|lt=Ise Shinto|ja|伊勢神道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Shinto|qq=}} (also known as Watarai Shinto). These texts include the following five scriptures:

* *The Record of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Amaterasu in Ise* (天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記)
* *The Biography of the Enshrinement of the Two Imperial Grand Shrines of Ise* (伊勢二所皇太神御鎮座伝記)
* *The Original Chronicle of the Enshrinement of the Imperial Grand Deity Toyouke* (豊受皇太神御鎮座本紀)
* *The Original Record of Treasures and Foundations of the Two Great Shrines of Ise* (造伊勢二所太神宮宝基本記)
* {{ill|The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Chronicle of Princess Yamato-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}

While the colophons of these texts claim origins dating back to before the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], it is widely believed that they were actually composed during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] by Watarai Yukitada and other priests of the Outer Shrine of Ise.

== Description ==
According to studies in {{ill|history|ja|歴史学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/history|1=study of history|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1066186}} and [[:en:Japanese literature|Japanese literature]], these texts were likely authored by members of the {{ill|Watarai clan|ja|度会氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watarai clan|qq=}}, who served as priests at the {{ill|Outer Shrine of Ise|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Outer Shrine of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}. Their objective was to elevate the status of the Outer Shrine to equal or even surpass that of the {{ill|Inner Shrine|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inner Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtai Jingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, which enshrines [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]. To achieve this, they identified {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyōke Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}} (the deity of the Outer Shrine) with both [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[:en:Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuni-no-Tokotachi]], thereby asserting Toyouke's universal divine supremacy over Amaterasu.<ref>{{Cite book|ja|title=偽書が揺るがせた日本史|date=2020-03-25|publisher=山川出版社|pages=82-84|author=原田 実}}</ref>

Although Ise Shinto was fundamentally based on the *{{ill|Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja|神本仏迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpon Butsujaku Setsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹|1=honji suijaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3080781}}* ("Shinto as the Source, Buddhism as the Trace") as a critique of the {{ill|honji suijaku|ja|本地垂迹説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji suijaku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 本地垂迹}} theory, the logical framework of these texts shows clear influences from Buddhist thought, especially from the [[:en:Shugendō|Shugendo]] tradition, as seen in works like *{{ill|The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|ja|大和葛城宝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|1=The Treasures of Mount Katsuragi in Yamato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433558}}*.

During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the *Five Texts of Shinto* faced harsh criticism from scholars such as {{ill|Yoshimi Yukikazu|ja|吉見幸和|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshimi Yukikazu|1=Yoshimi Yoshikazu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q63131320}}, a Shinto priest of the [[:en:Tōshō-gū|Toshogu Shrine]] in Owari Province, who denounced them as forgeries.{{Sfn|必携日本史用語}}
